sectionName: Biological & Biochemical Foundations of Living Systems

questions.passage: Cell biologists measured actin polymerization using pyrene-labeled actin. Fluorescence increases as actin monomers incorporate into filaments. Cells were treated with cytochalasin D, which caps growing actin ends, or phalloidin, which stabilizes actin filaments. The figure summarizes fluorescence over time.
questions.stem: Which treatment most directly inhibits addition of actin monomers to growing filaments?

explanations.A: Phalloidin stabilizes existing filaments rather than blocking polymerization in this setup.
explanations.B: Actin polymerization requires ATP-bound actin monomers.
explanations.C: Correct. Cytochalasin D caps barbed ends and prevents filament elongation, producing low fluorescence.
explanations.D: Collagen is an extracellular matrix protein, not an actin-end capping drug.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Actin polymerization assay under control, cytochalasin D, and phalloidin conditions.

questions.stem: Actin filaments are especially important for which cellular structure?

explanations.A: The mitotic spindle is primarily microtubule-based.
explanations.B: Correct. Microvilli are supported by actin bundles, unlike cilia/flagella, which use microtubules.
explanations.D: The nuclear lamina is composed mainly of intermediate filaments.

questions.stem: Which nucleotide state is most associated with newly added actin monomers?

explanations.A: Tubulin, not actin, binds GTP in microtubules.
explanations.B: cAMP is a signaling molecule, not the standard nucleotide bound by polymerizing actin.
explanations.C: NADH is not the nucleotide controlling actin polymerization.
explanations.D: Correct. ATP-actin adds preferentially to growing filament ends; ATP is later hydrolyzed within the filament.

questions.stem: Why does phalloidin produce high persistent fluorescence?

choices.A: It stabilizes F-actin and reduces depolymerization.
choices.C: It digests actin into amino acids
choices.D: It converts actin filaments into collagen fibrils

explanations.A: Correct. Phalloidin binds filamentous actin and prevents filament breakdown.
explanations.C: Protein digestion would reduce polymer signal.
explanations.D: Actin and collagen are distinct proteins in different cellular contexts.

questions.stem: Which motor protein moves along actin filaments in muscle contraction?

explanations.B: Dynein moves along microtubules, especially in cilia and retrograde transport.
explanations.C: DNA polymerase synthesizes DNA and is not a cytoskeletal motor.
explanations.D: Correct. Myosin heads bind actin and use ATP hydrolysis to generate force.

questions.passage: Researchers examined cancer cell migration through ECM-coated membranes. Cells were placed above membranes coated with collagen, fibronectin, laminin, or fibronectin plus excess RGD peptide. Some cells had integrin β1 knocked down. Migrated cells were counted after 12 hours.
questions.stem: Which ECM condition most strongly promoted migration of control cells?

explanations.A: Collagen supports some migration but less than fibronectin in the figure.
explanations.B: Laminin is not the highest condition for control cells in the figure.
explanations.C: Correct. The control cells show the highest migrated cell count on fibronectin.
explanations.D: Excess RGD reduces migration by competing for integrin binding.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. Migration of control and integrin β1 knockdown cells across ECM-coated membranes.

questions.stem: Why did excess RGD peptide reduce migration?

choices.B: It competed with ECM ligands for integrin binding.

explanations.A: Increased integrin expression would generally not explain reduced migration.
explanations.B: Correct. Many integrins bind RGD motifs in ECM proteins; soluble RGD can act as a competitive inhibitor.
explanations.C: RGD is a peptide motif, not a nucleic acid converter.
explanations.D: The migration effect is tied to integrin-ECM binding.

questions.stem: Which intracellular structure most directly generates protrusive force during migration?

choices.A: Polymerizing actin at the leading edge

explanations.A: Correct. Actin polymerization drives lamellipodia/filopodia extension during cell migration.
explanations.B: Chromatin state does not directly push the membrane forward.
explanations.C: Hemoglobin is not a migration motor.
explanations.D: Human cancer cells do not use bacterial flagella.

questions.stem: A β1 integrin knockdown most directly reduces migration by impairing:

explanations.A: Integrins primarily mediate adhesion, not ribosomal decoding.
explanations.B: That is a renal transport function unrelated to this migration assay.
explanations.C: Correct. Integrins are transmembrane adhesion receptors that link ECM to the cytoskeleton.
explanations.D: Lactate production is metabolic and not the direct integrin knockdown effect.

questions.stem: Which experiment would best test whether migration requires actin polymerization?

choices.A: Add DNase to the culture medium only
choices.B: Repeat the migration assay with cytochalasin D treatment.

explanations.A: DNase tests extracellular DNA involvement, not actin dynamics.
explanations.B: Correct. Cytochalasin D blocks actin filament elongation, directly testing the role of actin polymerization in migration.
explanations.C: lacZ reporter assays do not test mammalian cell migration.
explanations.D: Hemoglobin is not an ECM adhesion ligand.
